title: You Might Not Need 50 Diffusion Steps — Ziv Ilan, Nvidia
summary: NVIDIA demonstrated video diffusion generation at near real-time speeds on Blackwell B200 by combining quantization, caching, and step distillation to reduce diffusion steps from fifty to as few as one or four. The approach includes dynamic quantization with Black Forest Labs on Flux 2, a caching method that avoids recomputing unchanged latent chunks, and distillation methods using trajectory-based or distribution-based training. NVIDIA's open-source FastGen repository packages the post-training and GPU sharding work to apply these optimizations at scale.
writeup: Ziv Ilan of NVIDIA shows how to push video diffusion generation toward real-time speeds on Blackwell B200 by stacking three techniques: quantization, caching, and step distillation, cutting diffusion steps from fifty to as few as one or four. The specifics include dynamic quantization developed with Black Forest Labs on Flux 2, a caching method that skips recomputation of unchanged latent chunks, and distillation through trajectory-based or distribution-based training. The optimizations ship in NVIDIA's open-source FastGen repository, which packages the post-training and GPU sharding work to apply them at scale. The takeaway: most of the cost in diffusion inference comes from redundant steps and compute that targeted post-training can remove.
